title: Play-Based Movement & Bodyweight Foundations
pillar_name: Physical Literacy, Martial Arts & Human Performance

required.title: [Environment] Outdoor space with climbing opportunities and objects to carry

supplementary.title: [Video] YouTube: Zach Even-Esh (backyard strength training for kids)

competence_markers.description: The student can perform 10+ full push-ups (or equivalent on their progression path — knee push-ups count if full push-ups are not yet achievable, with upward trend)
The student can hang from a bar for 30+ seconds and is progressing toward their first pull-up (flexed-arm hangs, negatives)
The student can perform 20+ bodyweight squats to full depth with good form
The student can carry an object equal to 25% of their bodyweight for 200+ yards without setting it down
The student can sprint 40 yards with visible effort and good basic mechanics
The student can climb a rope or equivalent pulling challenge for at least 6 feet
The student can sustain physical work (carrying, digging, hauling) for 30+ minutes without quitting or significant performance loss
The student voluntarily participates in physical work and carrying tasks without being asked

cross_connections.description: Movement competence is the prerequisite for strength development. You cannot load a pattern you cannot perform.
Strength, grip, and endurance built here directly support grappling and striking training.
Carrying firewood, building shelter, hauling water — these are 2.4 skills that require 2.1.2 strength.
Regular training even when you do not feel like it is the purest form of discipline. The student who trains on the hard days is building IC2 in physical form.
Carrying something heavy when your arms are burning and your grip is failing is a controlled stress exposure. You learn that discomfort is not an emergency.

content.why_this_matters:
Strong people are harder to kill and more useful in general. That is not a metaphor. A person who can carry a heavy pack, lift something off someone who is trapped, drag a person to safety, or work all day without collapsing is a person who is genuinely useful when things go wrong. A weak person — no matter how smart, how kind, how well-intentioned — is a bystander in those moments.

Right now, you are not training with barbells or heavy weights. You are too young for that, and you do not need it yet. What you need is to build the broadest possible base of physical strength, endurance, and toughness through your own bodyweight, through carrying real things, through climbing and sprinting and working hard outside. You are building the raw material that structured training will shape later.

The stronger you are now, the more capable you will be in everything else: martial arts, survival skills, sport, manual work, and daily life. Strength is not vanity. It is the foundation of physical sovereignty.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Bodyweight strength foundations** — push-ups, pull-up progressions, squats, lunges, and crawling patterns that build functional strength
2. **Carrying capacity** — the ability to pick up heavy, awkward objects and move them over distance
3. **Climbing as strength training** — grip strength and pulling power built through ropes, trees, and walls
4. **Running and sprint capacity** — aerobic base and anaerobic power through play
5. **Work capacity** — the ability to sustain physical effort over extended periods through real tasks
6. **The understanding that strength serves a purpose** — you get strong to be useful, not to look impressive
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Your Body Is the First Barbell

Before you ever touch a weight, your own body provides all the resistance you need. Push-ups use your bodyweight as the load for your chest, shoulders, and triceps. Pull-ups use your bodyweight as the load for your back and biceps. Squats use your bodyweight as the load for your legs. These movements — pushing, pulling, and squatting — are the foundation of all strength training.

**Push-up progression:**
Start wherever you are. Every level is legitimate. No shame in starting at the beginning.
1. Wall push-ups — hands on the wall, lean in and push back
2. Incline push-ups — hands on a bench or step
3. Knee push-ups — knees on the ground, full push-up motion from the knees
4. Full push-ups — toes on the ground, body straight from head to heels, lower until your chest touches the ground, push back up
5. When full push-ups are easy, add volume — how many can you do in one set?

**Pull-up progression:**
1. Dead hang — just hang from the bar as long as you can
2. Flexed-arm hang — jump to the top of the pull-up, chin above the bar, hold as long as you can
3. Negative pull-ups — jump to the top, lower yourself down as slowly as possible (5-10 seconds)
4. Band-assisted pull-ups — a resistance band hooked to the bar supports some of your weight
5. Full pull-up — from dead hang, pull until your chin is above the bar

**Squat:**
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Sit back and down as if sitting in a chair. Go as deep as you can with heels on the ground. Stand back up. If you can squat all the way down with heels flat, you have good hip and ankle mobility. If you cannot, that is what you are working on.

**The standard at Stretching Branches:** You do not need to be doing sets and reps like a gym program. You need to be practicing these movements regularly — several times per week — and progressing. If you started at wall push-ups and now do full push-ups, that is real progress. If you could not hang for 10 seconds and now you hang for 30, that is real progress.

### Part 2: Carrying — The Most Useful Strength There Is

Every real-world use of strength involves picking something up and carrying it somewhere. Groceries. Firewood. Tools. Sandbags. An injured person. The ability to carry heavy, awkward objects over distance is the single most transferable strength skill.

**Farmer's walk:** Hold a heavy object in each hand — buckets of water, sandbags, heavy tools — and walk. Keep your chest up, shoulders back, core tight. Walk as far as you can without setting down. Rest. Go again. This trains your grip, your core, your legs, your shoulders, and your mental toughness simultaneously.

**Bear-hug carry:** Wrap your arms around something heavy and bulky — a sandbag, a bag of mulch, a large rock — and walk with it. This trains your entire body to work together under load.

**Overhead carry:** Hold something heavy above your head with straight arms and walk. Start light. This demands core stability and shoulder control.

**Partner carry:** Piggyback a sibling or training partner. Or learn the fireman's carry — the person drapes across your shoulders. This is both training and a practical survival skill.

**The carrying standard:** You should be carrying things regularly. Help carry everything. Volunteer for the heavy loads. The more you carry, the stronger you get.

### Part 3: Climbing as Strength Training

Climbing is not just a movement skill — it is one of the best strength-building activities available. Every time you climb a rope, you are doing pull-ups. Every time you traverse a wall, you are doing rows. Every time you grip a branch, you are training the grip strength that every other upper-body movement depends on.

**Rope climbing** is particularly valuable. A thick rope (1.5-inch manila) hung from a tree or beam requires grip endurance, pulling strength, and the coordination to use your feet for assistance. Start by just climbing hand-over-hand. Learn the J-hook foot technique to use your legs. Build to climbing the full length of the rope without stopping.

### Part 4: Running — Sprints, Not Just Jogging

Running is the most basic cardiovascular exercise, but how you run matters as much as how much you run.

**Sprinting** builds explosive power, fast-twitch muscle fibers, and anaerobic capacity. Sprint 30-60 yards at full speed. Walk back. Sprint again. Do 8-10 sprints. That is a sprint session. It takes 15 minutes and builds more athletic capacity than an hour of slow jogging.

**Play-based running** — tag, capture the flag, relay races, chasing games — is sprinting in disguise. You are changing direction, accelerating, decelerating, and sprinting in short bursts. This is the most sport-transferable conditioning there is.

**Trail running** — running on uneven ground builds ankle stability, proprioception, and the ability to adjust your stride to the terrain. Run on trails, through woods, up hills. The unpredictable surface is the training stimulus.

**Long slow running** has its place for building an aerobic base, but at Stretching Branches, play-based running and sprinting should dominate. The aerobic base develops naturally from high-volume outdoor play.

### Part 5: Work as Conditioning

Stacking firewood, digging holes, hauling buckets of water, moving rocks, shoveling dirt, carrying building materials — this is real work and it is real conditioning. A child who spends an hour helping split and stack firewood has done a conditioning session that is more functional than anything a gym can provide.

Work builds a quality called **work capacity** — the ability to sustain physical output over extended periods without significant performance degradation. This is arguably the most important physical quality for real life. A person with high work capacity can work all day. A person with low work capacity is exhausted by mid-morning.

**The principle:** Do not separate training from life. When there is physical work to do, do it. When you carry, carry with intention. When you dig, dig with effort. Every physical task is training.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Push-Up Progression
**Developing well:** Progressing through the push-up ladder. Form is reasonable — body relatively straight, chest reaches near the ground. 10+ full push-ups or strong progression toward them.

**Developing slowly:** Stuck at wall or incline push-ups for months. No visible progression. Or doing "push-ups" with 4-inch range of motion and sagging hips.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student practicing push-ups 3+ times per week? Bodyweight strength requires frequency. Is form the standard, or are they counting reps regardless of quality?

### 2. Pull-Up Progression
**Developing well:** Can hang 30+ seconds. Progressing through negatives or flexed-arm hangs. Working toward first pull-up with visible strength gains.

**Developing slowly:** Cannot hang for more than a few seconds. No progression in weeks.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the bar accessible for daily hanging? Has the student been taught negative pull-ups specifically? Many children do not intuitively attempt a slow negative — they need to be shown.

### 3. Carrying
**Developing well:** Carries 25%+ bodyweight for 200+ yards. Volunteers for carrying tasks. Grip holds. Posture is upright under load.

**Developing slowly:** Avoids carrying. Sets down frequently. Cannot maintain grip.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student carrying regularly? Grip strength develops quickly with practice. Are the loads appropriate — challenging but achievable?

### 4. Work Capacity
**Developing well:** Can sustain 30+ minutes of physical work without quitting. Maintains effort. Does not complain excessively (some complaining is normal).

**Developing slowly:** Quits after 10 minutes. Performance degrades rapidly. Avoids physical work.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the work appropriately scaled? A 5-year-old carrying full-size logs is not scaled appropriately. Is the student sleeping and eating enough to support physical output?
